---
title: "Working with HAML: Converting and Processing HLA Antibody Data"
subtitle: "Demo scripts for the HAML 1.0 specification"
author:
  - name: Vanessa W. Menard
    affiliation: Tulane University School of Medicine
  - name: Loren Gragert
    affiliation: Tulane University School of Medicine
  - name: Eric Spierings
    affiliation: University Medical Center Utrecht
  - name: Ben Matern
    affiliation: National Marrow Donor Program
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  warning: false
---

## Introduction

This document walks through three fundamental operations with HLA Antibody Markup Language (HAML):

1. **Convert** a laboratory CSV export into standardized HAML XML
2. **Analyze** the HAML file by applying an MFI threshold to classify beads
3. **Virtual Crossmatch** using HAML antibody data and donor HLA typing

Each section is self-contained and can be adapted for your own data.

### What is HAML?

HLA single antigen bead (SAB) assays are the standard technology for identifying HLA antibodies in transplant patients. Every transplant center in the US uses them. But the raw data, mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) values for each bead, is exported in vendor-specific CSV formats that differ between One Lambda (Thermo Fisher), Werfen (Immucor), and institutional laboratory information systems.

**HAML** (HLA Antibody Markup Language) is an XML-based standard that provides a single, vendor-neutral format for SAB assay results. It captures raw MFI values, bead metadata, assay kit information, adjusted calculations, and interpretation results. HAML was developed by the [IHIW](https://www.ihiw.org/) Clinical Histocompatibility Laboratory Informatics Working Group.

**Schema version:** This document targets the HAML 0.6.1 draft schema, which will become HAML 1.0 upon publication.

```{python}
#| label: setup
import sys
from pathlib import Path

REPO_ROOT = Path("..").resolve()
sys.path.insert(0, str(REPO_ROOT / "scripts"))

import pandas as pd
from lxml import etree
```

## Part 1: Converting SAB Data to HAML

The most common starting point is a CSV export from laboratory analysis software. Here we convert a One Lambda HLA Fusion export into HAML XML.

### Input: One Lambda Fusion CSV

The standard export has one row per bead with columns for sample ID, bead ID, HLA specificity, raw MFI, and bead count. Beads 1 and 2 are negative and positive controls (no specificity assigned).

```{python}
#| label: load-csv
csv_path = REPO_ROOT / "data" / "sample_sab_class1.csv"
df_input = pd.read_csv(csv_path)

print(f"Input: {csv_path.name}")
print(f"Beads: {len(df_input)} rows, {df_input['Sample ID'].nunique()} sample(s)\n")
df_input.head(10)
```

### Conversion

The converter maps each CSV row into the HAML document hierarchy. Each bead becomes a `target-bead-observation` element with `bead-info` (HLA specificity) and `bead-raw-data` (MFI value).

```{python}
#| label: convert
from csv_to_haml import read_fusion_csv, build_haml, validate_haml

beads = read_fusion_csv(csv_path)
root = build_haml(beads, reporting_center="HAML Demo Laboratory")

xml_bytes = etree.tostring(root, pretty_print=True, xml_declaration=True, encoding="UTF-8")
xml_str = xml_bytes.decode("utf-8")

lines = xml_str.split("\n")
print(f"Generated HAML XML: {len(lines)} lines\n")
print("\n".join(lines[:40]))
print(f"\n... ({len(lines) - 40} more lines)")
```

### Schema Validation

The XSD schema enforces structural correctness. Any HAML consumer can validate a file before processing.

```{python}
#| label: validate
is_valid, errors = validate_haml(root)
if is_valid:
    print("XSD Validation: PASSED")
else:
    print(f"XSD Validation: FAILED\n{errors}")
```

## Part 2: MFI Threshold Classification

Now we read the HAML file back and classify each bead by applying a 2,000 MFI threshold.

```{python}
#| label: analyze
from haml_analyzer import parse_haml_beads, classify_bead, extract_locus

output_dir = REPO_ROOT / "output"
output_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
haml_path = output_dir / "demo_class1.haml.xml"
with open(haml_path, "w") as f:
    f.write(xml_str)

all_beads = parse_haml_beads(haml_path)
target_beads = [b for b in all_beads if b["bead_type"] == "target"]

nc = next((b for b in all_beads if b["bead_type"] == "negative-control"), None)
pc = next((b for b in all_beads if b["bead_type"] == "positive-control"), None)

THRESHOLD = 2000

print(f"Target beads: {len(target_beads)}")
print(f"NC: {nc['raw_mfi']:.0f}  |  PC: {pc['raw_mfi']:.0f}  |  PC/NC: {pc['raw_mfi']/nc['raw_mfi']:.1f}")
print(f"Threshold: {THRESHOLD} MFI\n")

rows = []
for b in target_beads:
    rows.append({
        "specificity": b["hla_specificity"],
        "locus": extract_locus(b["hla_specificity"]),
        "raw_mfi": b["raw_mfi"],
        "classification": classify_bead(b["raw_mfi"], THRESHOLD),
    })
df = pd.DataFrame(rows)

positives = df[df["classification"] == "Positive"].sort_values("raw_mfi", ascending=False)
print(f"Positive: {len(positives)}  |  Borderline: {(df['classification']=='Borderline').sum()}  |  Negative: {(df['classification']=='Negative').sum()}\n")
positives[["specificity", "locus", "raw_mfi"]]
```

### MFI Distribution

```{python}
#| label: fig-mfi-distribution
#| fig-cap: "MFI distribution across all Class I beads with threshold classification"

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as ticker

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

colors = {"Positive": "#c0392b", "Borderline": "#f39c12", "Negative": "#7f8c8d"}
ax = axes[0]
for cls in ["Negative", "Borderline", "Positive"]:
    subset = df[df["classification"] == cls]
    ax.hist(subset["raw_mfi"], bins=30, alpha=0.7, label=cls, color=colors[cls])
ax.axvline(x=THRESHOLD, color="#2c3e50", linestyle="--", linewidth=2, label=f"Threshold ({THRESHOLD})")
ax.axvline(x=THRESHOLD*0.5, color="#95a5a6", linestyle=":", linewidth=1, label=f"Borderline ({int(THRESHOLD*0.5)})")
ax.set_xlabel("Raw MFI")
ax.set_ylabel("Number of Beads")
ax.set_title("MFI Distribution")
ax.legend(fontsize=8)
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(ticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"{x:,.0f}"))

ax2 = axes[1]
summary = df.groupby("locus")["classification"].value_counts().unstack(fill_value=0)
summary = summary.reindex(columns=["Positive", "Borderline", "Negative"], fill_value=0)
summary.plot(kind="barh", stacked=True, ax=ax2, color=[colors["Positive"], colors["Borderline"], colors["Negative"]])
ax2.set_xlabel("Number of Beads")
ax2.set_title("Classification by HLA Locus")
ax2.legend(fontsize=8)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Part 3: Simple Virtual Crossmatch

A virtual crossmatch (VXM) predicts whether a patient's antibodies will react against a specific donor's HLA antigens. The basic logic: any positive antibody matching a donor allele is a donor-specific antibody (DSA), predicting a positive crossmatch.

```{python}
#| label: vxm
from simple_vxm import read_donor_typing, identify_dsa, predict_vxm

donor_path = REPO_ROOT / "data" / "sample_donor_typing.txt"
donor_alleles = read_donor_typing(donor_path)

print("Donor HLA Typing:")
for allele in sorted(donor_alleles):
    print(f"  {allele}")
```

```{python}
#| label: vxm-results
# Class I DSA
dsa_c1 = identify_dsa(all_beads, donor_alleles, threshold=THRESHOLD)

# Class II: convert, parse, find DSA
csv_path_c2 = REPO_ROOT / "data" / "sample_sab_class2.csv"
beads_c2 = read_fusion_csv(csv_path_c2)
root_c2 = build_haml(beads_c2, reporting_center="HAML Demo Laboratory", hla_class="II", catalog="LS2A01")
haml_c2 = output_dir / "demo_class2.haml.xml"
with open(haml_c2, "w") as f:
    f.write(etree.tostring(root_c2, pretty_print=True, xml_declaration=True, encoding="UTF-8").decode())
dsa_c2 = identify_dsa(parse_haml_beads(haml_c2), donor_alleles, threshold=THRESHOLD)

all_dsa = dsa_c1 + dsa_c2

print(f"MFI Threshold: {THRESHOLD}\n")
if all_dsa:
    dsa_df = pd.DataFrame(all_dsa).sort_values("raw_mfi", ascending=False)
    print(f"Donor-Specific Antibodies ({len(all_dsa)} found):\n")
    print(dsa_df[["specificity", "locus", "raw_mfi", "match_type"]].to_string(index=False))

print(f"\nVirtual Crossmatch Prediction: {predict_vxm(all_dsa)}")
```

## Discussion

This notebook demonstrated the core value of HAML: **a single workflow that works regardless of which laboratory produced the data**. The same analyzer and VXM scripts work on HAML files generated from any vendor's CSV export, because the HAML schema standardizes the representation.

### What This Demo Does NOT Cover

Clinical antibody interpretation involves locus-specific thresholds, cross-reactive group (CREG) analysis, artifact detection (prozone, complement interference), historical antibody patterns, platform concordance, and eplet-level molecular analysis. These capabilities require domain-specific algorithms beyond simple threshold classification.

### Next Steps

- **HAML 1.0 finalization:** The schema used here (0.6.1 draft) includes new cell-based assay support for flow crossmatch and CDC data.
- **Adjusted MFI and interpretations:** HAML supports multiple adjusted-data entries and interpretation entries per bead, enabling laboratories to share analytical reasoning alongside raw data.
- **IHIW Converters:** Production-grade converters are maintained at [github.com/IHIW/Converters](https://github.com/IHIW/Converters).
